segments.s184-g17: وزان (archaic compound = وز + آن) here functions adverbially 'from that side/thereupon'; روی is 'side/direction' (classical Persian word order puts the direction after the particle). منزل بمنزل is a repetitive adverbial phrase 'from one station/place to the next' (منزل = 'stage/station') and سپاه is the noun 'army' that this whole adverbial phrase modifies.
segments.s184-g18: همی رفت is the habitual/progressive past (همی + past stem) meaning 'kept going' or 'proceeded'; و simply links clauses. پیش‌اندر آمد is literal 'came before/in front of' (پیش‌اندر = 'in front of, before') with آمد 'came' (past), and راه is the object 'road(s)'. Together: 'and two roads came up before them.'
segments.s184-g19: ز یک‌سو is a locative phrase 'from/on one side' (ز = poetic 'from', یک‌سو = 'one side'). بیابان is 'desert/wilderness' and بی‌آب is the adjectival compound 'waterless'; و نم links 'waterless' to نم ('moisture/dew')—the phrase describes the sterility of that side.
segments.s184-g20: کلات is a place-name (or a fortress) marking the opposite side; از دگر سوی is 'from the other side' (از = from, دگر = other, سوی = side/toward). و راه چرم links another feature: راه 'road' qualified by چرم 'leathern, of leather' (here a descriptive name or characteristic).
segments.s184-g21: بماندند is the past plural 'they remained/they stayed' (the prefix ب- is common in Ferdowsi's narrative past forms). بر جای = 'at the place/position of' and پیلان is the genitive plural of پیل 'elephant' (here literal 'the elephants'); و کوس adds 'and the war-drum' as another location/landmark where they waited.
segments.s184-g22: بدان is the archaic locative 'to/at that' (here 'there'); تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'until' and is followed by بیاید, a subjunctive/present form 'may/should come/arrive' used here in the temporal sense 'until ... arrives'. سپهدار طوس is the noun phrase 'Commander Tūs' (سپهدار = commander, طوس = Tūs).
segments.s184-g23: کدامین is the interrogative 'which (one)' modifying پسند 'choice/liking'. آیدش is the verb آید 'comes' with the enclitic -ش 'to him', an older word order (verb before its clitic), so the clause literally asks 'Which choice comes to him of these two roads?' i.e. 'Which of the two roads would he choose?'. زین here is the archaic contraction corresponding to 'از این' ('of/from these'), but that specific sense is not listed in the provided sense set (so marked -1). راه is 'road'.
segments.s184-g24: بفرمان is an ablative/agentive phrase 'by (the) command'. رود here is an archaic verbal form (from رفتن) meaning 'went/took (the road)' — in Ferdowsi the form رود can appear as a jussive/past narrative; this verbal sense is not present in the provided noun senses (so marked -1). هم = 'also/likewise', بران ره = 'on/upon that way' (بران = 'on that/thereon', ره = 'way/road'), and سپاه is the subject 'army' (the clause order is somewhat flexible: 'by command the army likewise went that way').
segments.s184-g1: سپهبد (spahbed) is the subject; بشد تیز is an archaic/periphrastic past construction: بشد (ب + شد) with تیز functions as 'became swift' or more idiomatically 'hastened'. و links the two actions. برگشت is a past tense verb 'returned' and شاه follows it (verb–subject inversion, common in poetic Persian).
segments.s184-g2: سوی is a preposition 'toward' and takes کاخ as its object 'toward the palace'. با رستم و با سپاه are prepositional phrases 'with Rostam and with the army' (با is repeated for rhythmic/emphatic balance); this whole phrase attaches to برگشت ('returned toward the palace with ...').
segments.s184-g3: یکی here is an indefinite 'a certain' (introducing an event); مجلس آراست is a verb phrase 'arranged/set up a council' (آراست is past of آراستن 'to arrange/embellish'). با پیلتن names the place/setting—in older/poetic usage با can mark accompaniment or location, so 'at/with Piltan' (پیلتن is a place-name).
segments.s184-g4: This is a coordination (a list of participants): رد (here likely a proper name or title, ambiguous), و موبد ('and the mobed' — a Zoroastrian priest), و خسرو رای زن ('and Khosrow, rāy-zan' where رای + زن functions as 'giver of counsel' = 'counsellor'). رای زن is a compound in which رای ('opinion/counsel') combines with زن (agentive 'one who...') to mean 'counsellor'.
segments.s184-g5: فراوان modifies the following verb phrase as an adverb 'much'/'at length'. سخن گفت is a verb phrase 'spoke' (سخن as 'speech' and گفت from گفتن 'to say'); ز افراسیاب uses poetic ز = 'about'/'concerning' to mark the topic 'about Afrasiab'.
segments.s184-g6: This repeats the topical preposition ز ('about') for successive objects: ز رنج تن خویش = 'about the suffering (رنج) of his body (تن خویش)'. خویش is formed from خوی + ش (possessive) to mean 'his own'. وز here is the poetically contracted form (و + ز) meaning 'and (about)'. درد باب is another topical phrase 'the pain of باب' — باب in this context is ambiguous (could be a name or a rare sense), so the exact referent is uncertain from the line alone.
segments.s184-g7: ز introduces a cause/reason here ('because of' / 'concerning'); آزردن is the infinitive 'to hurt/ offend' used nominally ('the offending/hurt'); مادر پارسا is a noun phrase 'the pious mother' with پارسا as an adjectival epithet.
segments.s184-g8: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that / who / namely'); با ما چه کرد is the core clause 'what he did to us' (با ما = 'to/with us', چه کرد = 'what did (he) do'); آن بد پرجفا is a descriptive nominal group 'that evil/cruel one' — پرجفا is a compound adjective meaning intensely injurious or cruel, applied to آن.
segments.s184-g33: A verbless descriptive phrase listing the landscape on both sides: 'چپ و راست' ('left and right') joined by و, followed by two nouns 'آباد' (inhabited/prosperous places) and 'آب روان' (lit. 'water flowing' = running water). Word order is coordinate: location ('left and right') then the things found there; 'آب روان' is a noun + adjective ('flowing').
segments.s184-g34: A rhetorical question. 'بیابان چه جوییم' = 'what (shall) we seek in the desert?' with 'جوییم' in the present-subjunctive/indicative sense 'we seek/search for'. 'و رنج روان' is added in coordination: 'and (the) hardship of travel' (روان qualifying رنج).
segments.s184-g35: 'مرا بود' is an impersonal/have-like construction meaning 'it was to me' or 'I happened to have'; 'روزی' = 'one day / once'; 'بدین ره گذر' = 'to pass by this way/along this road' (ب + دین = 'to this', ره = 'road/way', گذر = 'passing/passage'). Together they mean 'one day it befell me to pass this way.'
segments.s184-g36: 'چو' here is temporal 'when'; 'گژدهم' is a proper name (the person referenced); 'پیش سپه راهبر' is a prepositional/positional phrase meaning 'as leader/in front of the host' or 'leading the army' (پیش = before/in front of, سپه = host/army, راهبر = guide/leader). The whole line: 'when G. was leader in front of the army.'
segments.s184-g37: 'ندیدیم' is the past negative 'we did not see' (ن- + دیدیم). 'از این راه' = 'from/by this way/route'. 'رنجی دراز' = 'any long hardship' (رنج with the indefinitizing suffix -ی, followed by adjective دراز 'long'). The clause states absence of prolonged suffering on that road.
segments.s184-g38: Contrastive/exceptive clause: 'مگر بود' = 'except there was' or 'save that there was'; 'لختی' = 'for a short while'; 'نشیب و فراز' = 'a descent and an ascent' (i.e., some ups and downs). The clause means 'except for a brief descent and ascent.'
segments.s184-g39: 'بدو گفت' = 'to him (he) said' (به + او + گفت) — a dative + verb of saying. 'گودرز پرمایه شاه' is an appositive identifying the speaker: 'Goudarz, the king's substantial/wealthy one' or 'Goudarz, the king's valued man' (پرمایه qualifying Goudarz in relation to the king).
segments.s184-g40: 'ترا پیش‌رو کرد پیش سپاه' = literal 'he made you پیش‌رو before the host.' 'ترا' = 'you' (object with را), 'پیش‌رو' = 'vanguard/forerunner' or 'one who goes ahead', 'کرد' = past 'made/did', and 'پیش سپاه' = 'in front of the army' (locative). Word order is object–predicate–locative (typical Persian ordering in reported speech).
segments.s184-g25: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آمد is the past tense verb 'came'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon/before' linking the verb to its object. سرکشان here is a plural substantive (contextually 'the rebellious/insolent ones' or 'the chiefs'), but the provided lemma senses don't match the poetic use, so its precise lexical sense is flagged below. طوس is the proper name 'Tūs' and نرم is an adjectival epithet 'gentle' modifying طوس; the order places the prepositional phrase before the name for emphasis.
segments.s184-g26: سخن گفت is a common verb phrase 'spoke / said (words)' with the noun سخن preceding the verb گفت (object before verb). ازان راه is a prepositional phrase 'about/from that road' (ازان = 'of/from that', راه = 'road/path'), and بی‌آب و گرم are two postnominal adjectives joined by و describing that راه: 'waterless and hot'.
segments.s184-g27: بگودرز is a prepositional phrase 'to Goudarz' (ب- prefix marking the addressee). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here 'he said to Goudarz'). این بیابان خشک is a nominal phrase 'this dry wilderness' where این is the demonstrative, بیابان the noun, and خشک the attributive adjective following the noun.
segments.s184-g28: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. گرد عنبر is a noun phrase 'the dust/cloud of amber(gris)' (گرد as 'dust' or 'particle', عنبر here likely 'ambergris' in poetic context). دهد is a 3rd-person verb 'gives' (here in a potential/optative sense), and باد is used as an optative particle 'may' (a poetic wish/possibility marker) rather than its literal sense 'wind'. مشک is the object 'musk'. The clause therefore speculates conditionally about scent coming from amber-dust.
segments.s184-g29: چو is the temporal/conditional 'when/if'. رانیم is a 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive form 'we drive/ride/set out' (used here as a hortatory/conditional 'if we ride'). روزی functions adverbially 'one day' or 'once', and به تندی دراز is an adverbial phrase 'with great speed for a long distance' (به تندی = 'quickly', دراز = 'far/long').
segments.s184-g30: بآب و بآسایش are prepositional/adverbial phrases ('with water and with ease/comfort') formed by the prefix بـ- meaning 'with' or 'having'. آید is the verb 'comes' (here future/habitual sense), and نیاز is the noun 'need/relief/longing' functioning as what will arrive or be satisfied. Together the clause says that water and comfort will arrive to meet their need.
segments.s184-g31: همان به که is an idiomatic classical phrase meaning 'it is best/it is preferable that'. به is part of the construction linking to the following subordinate clause (که). سوی Kِلات و چرم is a directional phrase 'toward Kalat and Chirm' (سوی = 'toward', کلات a place-name; چرم here functions as a place-name in context rather than the common noun 'leather').
segments.s184-g32: برانیم is a 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive 'let us drive/ride' or 'we should drive/lead'. و connects the two coordinated actions. منزل کنیم is 'let us make camp/settle for the night' (منزل here = a stage or lodging on a journey, کنیم = 'we do/make'). از is the preposition 'from' or 'away from' and می‌م appears as a lexical item in the manuscript (here treated as an existing predicate or locative element in context); grammatically the clause expresses movement/establishment of camp 'from/away from X'.
segments.s184-g9: Word order is object (مرا + را) before verb: مرا (me + object marker را) is the direct object and داد is the past tense verb 'gave/handed'. زی is a poetic preposition (variant of ز/از) linking the recipient group (شبانان بی‌مایه) to the verb: 'gave me to/among the worthless shepherds.' The subject is omitted and understood from the narrative context.
segments.s184-g10: ز من here is a prepositional phrase 'from me/of me' (ز = from). کس before the verb ندانست functions in the sense 'no one' (negative indefinite used with a verb), and ندانست is past tense 'did not know' / 'knew not'. نام و نژاد are coordinated objects 'name and lineage' — what nobody knew about the speaker.
segments.s184-g11: فرستادم is 1st-person past 'I sent/dispatched.' این بار is a temporal phrase 'this time/occasion' modifying the sending. طوس و سپاه are the direct objects of فرستادم (Tus and an army); the conjunction و simply links them.
segments.s184-g12: ازین پس is a fixed adverbial phrase 'from now on' (از + این + پس). من و تو is the coordinated subject 'you and I' (word order 'I and you' is idiomatic), and گذاریم is a first‑person plural present/subjunctive/jussive form meaning 'we will place/take / we shall set out' followed by راه 'the road' as its object — 'we will take the road.'
segments.s184-g13: آوریم is the 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive 'we bring/produce/bring about.' The structure جهان بر بداندیش تنگ آوریم is idiomatic: بر + [target] = 'against/to the harm of', تنگ آوردن = 'to make narrow / to oppress/harass.' So literally 'we will bring the world narrow upon the ill‑minded' → 'we will make life hard for malcontents.'
segments.s184-g14: This clause repeats the verb آوریم. سر دشمنان is the direct object 'the heads of the enemies' (سر = head), and زیر سنگ is a prepositional phrase 'under stone' (under/beneath a rock) indicating the fate inflicted on those heads; the verb 'آوریم' again conveys 'we will bring / place'.
segments.s184-g15: ورا at the start is an indirect object/object‑direction 'to him' (or 'to that one'), so the order 'ورا پیلتن گفت' is inverted: 'to him Piltan said.' گفت is the past tense 'said,' followed by the reported imperative/adjuration کین غم مدار. کین here functions as 'this'/'that' (poetic demonstrative) or as 'grievance/sorrow,' and مدار is an imperative negative meaning 'do not hold/cherish' — together: 'do not cherish this sorrow.'
segments.s184-g16: به کام تو گردد is a conventional optative/blessing construction: به کام تو = 'to/for your desire' and گردد (subjunctive/optative of گردیدن) = 'may it become / may it turn out.' همه روزگار 'all the days/time' is the temporal scope: 'may all your days be as you desire.' Word order is typical Persian inversion for blessings.
segments.s184-g49: همان به که functions as a fixed archaic phrase meaning 'it is better that' or 'best that'. لشکر is the direct object 'the army', and بدین سو is a directional phrase ('in this direction' = بـ + دین/این + سو). بریم is a first-person-plural subjunctive/optative of بردن ('let us take/lead'); the clause as a whole expresses a proposal: 'it is better that we lead the army this way.'
segments.s184-g50: This is a coordinated continuation: بیابان 'the wilderness/desert' + و 'and' + فرسنگها 'parasangs (long distances)'. نشمریم is ن + شمریم (present-subjunctive/optative of شمردن) giving a negative jussive 'let us not count' or 'we will not reckon'. Together: 'let us go into the wilderness and not count the parasangs.'
segments.s184-g51: بدین گفته بودند is a circumstantial/perfect-past expression: بدین ('with/ by this') + گفته بودند (pluperfect/perfect: 'they had said' or 'having said'). همداستان is a predicate adjective meaning 'of one mind' or 'unanimous', linked to بودند as the past copula ('they were unanimous').
segments.s184-g52: This short clause gives the circumstance at the place: برین (بر + ین/این) and the following بر create a directional notion 'toward that place' or 'on that road'; نزد means 'near/by' and’ici نیز کس داستان' expresses that there was no person present for the tale—i.e. 'there was no one near to whom the story could be told.' Grammatically, کس here functions as an indefinite/negative sense ('no one' in this context) governed by نزد.
segments.s184-g53: براندند is a third-person-plural past tense 'they drove/moved/led'. ازان راه means 'along/from that road' (از + آن). پیلان is the plural 'elephants' (classical plural suffix -ان), and کوس are war-drums — these are coordinated objects or accompanying elements showing how they proceeded.
segments.s184-g54: بفرمان is a prepositional phrase 'by the command of' (equivalent to modern به فرمان). رای here means 'counsel/plan' and is coordinated with فرمان: 'by the command and counsel of ...'. سپهدار طوس is a title + name ('the commander Tūs'), the whole phrase modifying the verb (they moved) indicating who ordered/advised the action.
segments.s184-g41: بران ره is an adverbial phrase: بران (here) = 'on/along that' + ره = 'way/road'. که is the complementizer 'that'. گفت او = 'he said' (verb + subject). سپه را بران is a reported imperative: سپه را (the army, with را marking the definite object) + بران (here used adverbially/verbally to mean 'send/lead/drive (thence)'). Word order places the reported order after گفت او.
segments.s184-g42: نباید is an impersonal modal meaning 'it should not / must not be'; که introduces the subordinate clause. آید (present/subjunctive) = 'come/happen' in an impersonal construction; کسی را زیان = 'harm to anyone' (کسی = 'anyone', را marking the affected argument, زیان = 'harm/detriment'). The whole clause means 'that harm should not come to anyone.'
segments.s184-g43: نباید که introduces a subordinate clause of prohibition ('it should not be that ...'). گردد (from گردیدن) = 'become' (subjunctive/present) and دل‌آزرده is the predicate adjective 'saddened / offended'; شاه is the subject placed after the predicate (classical Persian word order). Together: 'the king should not become saddened.'
segments.s184-g44: بد آید is an impersonal expression 'bad/unfavorable will come' (بد = 'bad/unfavorable', آید = 'comes'); ز = 'from / because of' (poetic form), آزار او = 'his displeasure / hurt' (آزار = harm/annoyance, او = 'his'), بر سپاه = 'upon the army' (بر = 'on/upon'). The clause warns that the army will suffer from the king's displeasure.
segments.s184-g45: بدو = 'to him' (dative). گفت = 'he said'. طوس‌ای is a vocative 'Tūs!' with the -ای vocative particle. گو here is an addressing/performative element tied to the speech (literally 'say' / a vocative marker in this line), and نامدار is an epithet 'renowned' attached to the name. The phrase is a direct address: 'O Tūs, renowned one.'
segments.s184-g46: ازین گونه اندیشه = 'this kind of thought' (از/ازین = 'from/this', گونه = 'kind/manner', اندیشه = 'thought'). در دل = 'in (the) heart'. مدار here is a prohibitive/imperative 'do not keep/harbor' (classical poetic negative imperative), so the clause means 'do not harbor such thoughts in your heart.'
segments.s184-g47: کزین = 'from this' (prepositional phrase). شاه را uses را to topicalize/mark 'the king'; دل is the heart (subject), نگردد (from گشتن/گردیدن) = 'does not become' (negative present/future sense), and دژم = 'sullen/grim' as predicative adjective. The clause says 'From this the king's heart will not become sullen.'
segments.s184-g48: سزدگر is a conditional/optative expression 'if it befit / it is fitting' (here introducing a hypothetical). نداری = 'lack/poverty' (the assumed ill), روان = 'soul/spirit', جفت غم = 'companion/mate is sorrow' (جفت = pair/companion, غم = sorrow). The sense: 'If poverty comes, sorrow will be the soul's companion.'

sentences.s184-s9: From that side the army moved from station to station, and two roads lay before them.
sentences.s184-s10: On one side a waterless desert with no moisture; from the other side (came) Kalat and the leather road.
sentences.s184-s11: They remained at the place of the elephants and the drums until Commander Tūs should arrive.
sentences.s184-s12: Which choice pleases him of these two roads? By command, the army likewise took that road.
sentences.s184-s1: The spahbed hastened, and the king returned toward the palace with Rostam and with the army.
sentences.s184-s2: He set up a council at Piltan, with Rad, the mobed, and Khosrow the counsellor.
sentences.s184-s3: He spoke at length about Afrasiab, about the suffering of his body, and about the pain of (the) Bab.
sentences.s184-s4: Because of having wronged the pious mother — what that cruel, injurious one did to us.
sentences.s184-s17: On the left and right were inhabited places and flowing water; what do we seek in the desert and the toil of travel?
sentences.s184-s18: It happened to me one day to pass this road.
sentences.s184-s19: We did not see from this route any long suffering,
sentences.s184-s20: To him Goudarz, the king's rich (one), said: 'He made you the vanguard before the army.'
sentences.s184-s13: When gentle Tous came before the rebellious ones, he spoke of that waterless, hot road.
sentences.s184-s14: He said to Goudarz: 'This wilderness is dry; if the dust of amber gives (it) scent, (then) the wind (may give) musk.'
sentences.s184-s15: If we once ride swiftly for a long stretch, water and ease will come to relieve our need.
sentences.s184-s16: It is better that we head toward Kalat and Chirm and make camp from there.
sentences.s184-s5: They handed me over to worthless shepherds; from me no one knew my name or lineage.
sentences.s184-s6: This time I sent Tūs and an army; from now on you and I will take the road.
sentences.s184-s7: We will make the world narrow for the ill‑minded; we will bring the enemies' heads beneath stone.
sentences.s184-s8: To him Piltan said: 'Do not cherish this sorrow; may all your days go according to your desire.'
sentences.s184-s25: It is better that we lead the army in this direction; let us go into the wilderness and not count the parasangs.
sentences.s184-s26: With these words they were unanimous; there was no one nearby to tell the story.
sentences.s184-s27: They moved along that road with elephants and war-drums, by the command and counsel of the commander Tūs.
sentences.s184-s21: He said, "Send the army along that road — no one should suffer harm."
sentences.s184-s22: The king must not become saddened; his displeasure would bring misfortune upon the army.
sentences.s184-s23: He said to him, "O Tūs, renowned one, do not entertain such thoughts in your heart."
sentences.s184-s24: From this the king's heart will not become sullen; if poverty comes, sorrow will be the soul's companion.
